speaker: interviewer
text: [? --Franzman, ?] interviewing Paul Oppenheimer, in Solihull, England. Paul, what else do you remember about Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We-- we remember that, basically, it was not such a bad camp-- especially in retrospect what happened later. For example, in-- in Belsen, I had to work. The demarcation line in most camps seemed to be around 15, 16, and I was sometimes above it, sometimes below it. But in Westerbork, I had to work. But the work was not very arduous. I think I had to pick potatoes in the fields, and things like that. But I-- I had an arbeitskarte-- which I have here, amongst my collection of documents-- which clearly says, Paul Oppenheimer, arbeitskarter, Westerbork, barrack number 68. And on the other side of it, in pencil, is written down all the days when I was excused from work. And you will say-- see the date, and then it says, two days, cough-- or whatever it is, or something like that. Can't really decipher it all. But you see quite clearly, the two days, three days, et cetera. And I seem to have had lots of coughs and headaches and all sorts of illnesses, because when I've added it all up, I think it's something like 47 days I was excused from work, during the seven or eight months that we stayed in Westerbork. So that-- that is an interesting document which reminds me that I definitely did work. There's a lot of argument about what the age limit was in various camps, about who had to work, and who had not had to work. So I certainly worked in Westerbork. And obviously, my parents worked in Westerbork. I-- I don't remember what they did, but I don't believe that it was very arduous kind of work in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there doctors there, for that cough of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were, there was excellent medicine-- medical facilities. There was a hospital, and some of the best doctors. Because Dutch Jewish doctors, they-- they would be deported to Westerbork, and they would be kept back. People like that were also kept back-- people who-- who were good dentists or good doctors, and they would provide the facilities in Westerbork. Another category of people-- I must come back to my story in a minute-- but another category of people that were kept back in Westerbork were people who were good artists, musicians, et cetera, because they had an orchestra there. They had cinema. They had-- what's it called, can't think of the name now-- but anyway, theater shows. And all those sort of things, because some of the best artists from-- from Holland were Jews, and all stayed in Westerbork. And they were kept back, kind of thing-- not sent for deportation, so they could entertain us, kind of thing. So that was all available.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you remember going to any theater there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but it was all available. And we could go for walks, we could play football in Westerbork. So generally, it wasn't really so bad. There was no official school that we remember, or official Jewish instruction. But there probably was unofficially schools, and-- and religious instruction. The problem was that the people of the right age to give the instruction were working during the day, so they would have to do it in the evening. I mean, the other thing was there was plenty of food in-- in Westerbork. There was no problem there, nobody died starvation. In fact, nobody died in Westerbork at all, except from normal causes, as you would normally from various illnesses. There were no diseases, nothing like that in Westerbork. We could write letters and postcards, and again, I have samples of those that my parents wrote to non-Jewish friends in Amsterdam and elsewhere in Holland. And we recovered these letters after the war-- they gave them back to us. So I have all those documentary evidence of where we were, and which barracks we lived in, in Westerbork. And what we asked for-- we-- my parents seemed to ask for syrup, which is a cough mixture, quite a lot. In one letter they asked for pajamas, and in another letter for various other things. And we-- we were allowed to have food parcels and clothing parcels and all those things in Westerbork. So all in all, it wasn't really all that bad. The other thing we have, which is peculiar-- and not many people seem to have all these documents. In fact, I've never seen anybody else who has these documents-- is my [NON-ENGLISH] which is the bath card. And it shows that I had to have a bath at Friday afternoon, at 3 o'clock, every week. And not only was that regulated-- so there was a bath house, and I had to report there 3 o'clock every Friday afternoon-- but it got stamped. The card was stamped on the back on the 1st of January, on the 14th of January, the 7th of January, the 21st of January, 1944. The card was stamped every time to say that I had my bath properly. And my family after the war were very impressed to hear I had a bath every week, kind of thing. It was better than I do nowadays, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they communal baths?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what they were. I wouldn't have thought so-- no, I think we went into individual tubs, or maybe there were showers. Or I remember-- I see on the plan, there's a bath house in Westerbork, so that's obviously where we reported, and where we had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say communal bath, you mean all sitting together in a great big pool? Like they do in Japan? No, I'm sure it wasn't that-- I would have remembered that. I would say it was an individual tub, or it was a shower. And what were the living-- what were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in three-tiered bunks. And they were metal constructed three-tier bunks. And there were quite a lot of people living in the individual barracks. I think it was reasonably crowded-- not as crowded as Belsen later on, but yes, they-- it was probably not all that good, the sleeping accommodation kind of thing. But I think we had our meals together-- either in-- in either one of our barracks, or there were other dining halls, or something like that, where we perhaps had the meals together. And we had plenty of time to see each other, despite the fact that my parents worked and I worked. And Rudy and Eve presumably hung around the camp all day, maybe they had some instructions here and there. So I think we've covered most of my recollections from Westerbork. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was then in January '44 when we, apparently, were told that we were going to be sent to another camp for-- where all the exchange people, kind of all the people with blue stamps from Westerbork. And there was one transport in the middle of January-- we were not on that one. But then then there was-- on the 1st of February was another transport. We could see, straight away, that it was different and better. Because the train that came in was passenger coaches, not cattle trucks. And-- and things like that mean an awful lot to you-- little things like that tend to immediately suggests various things. And obviously, we could see that we were in a better situation than the people who had gone on the cattle trucks before. And so that night, the lists were read out, and we were all on the lists-- and-- and father and me in our barrack, and mother, Eve, and Rudy in the other barrack, they were all on the list. And we got together later on. And next morning, we all appeared, and we all went on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew we were going to Bergen-Belsen, because that apparently had been mentioned, discussed, earlier. In-- in January, in Westerbork, we were all told that we were going to go to a camp called-- called Bergen-Belsen, and it was all these exchange people. So we assumed that it wouldn't be too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the name mean anything to you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had no idea. We'd never heard of the name, Bergen-Belsen, before. Perhaps I-- I should mention that I have read that some people from Westerbork actually went to another camp in France, called Vittel, and these were people who had British, mainly American, passports. We didn't really notice that-- whether my parents noticed it, I-- I don't know. But this may have been the difference between them actually have passports and us only having a birth certificate. Because we can't understand why they were sent to Vittel, and-- and we were not. And we were sent to Bergen-Belsen, which was much worse, because the people in Vittel apparently all got out. And Vittel apparently was a very good camp-- it was like a hotel. So we've heard after the war. But I don't understand-- all sorts of things went on all the time where we never really figured out why, or what was happening. It was all via some underground rumors, kind of things, about what people thought was happening. Anyway, we went to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories do you have of the journey there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was with you in the compartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume that we were all together in the compartment. And it was, again, not a very long ride, because Westerbork is very much in the east of Holland-- northeast or Holland-- quite close to the German border. And Belsen isn't that all that far into Germany on that side. So I-- I can't remember how long the journey took. I can't even remember whether we arrived by day or night. But we arrived at this railway siding. And-- but we could see straight away that it wasn't really as gentle as Westerbork-- it all looked a lot harsher, even at the railway siding because there were SS there with dogs, and machine guns, and all that sort of thing. And we all had to line up, and we were walked to the camp-- which was a couple of miles, maybe. But we were pretty fit in-- in those days, really. We hadn't starved in any way. And-- and we had no disease amongst us, really. So it wasn't really difficult to-- to do the walk to the camp. And-- and when we saw the camp-- well, like Westerbork, it had watchtowers and barbed wire. Westerbork actually add a moat, but there was not moat in Bergen-Belsen. But there was more barbed wire, and probably more strict security than we were used to at Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Eve walk with you from the ramp to Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so, yeah. I would think so. I think women and children might have gone on the truck, or something like that. But she was then-- '44, '36, seven years old. I would have thought so, but I'm not sure, not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as you know, we've been back now. We've see where the railway siding is, and we've seen where the camp was, and it's not all that far. And compared to all the other things that we've been through, I would have thought that was a relatively easy journey. And the conditions we were then-- it was a lot worse when we came back. But at that time, it wasn't too difficult. Did you have to register there?

words.text: we've been back now. We've see

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no registration. We were herded into a-- a section of the camp in Belsen-- Belsen was a very large camp. Westerbork was also a very large camp, but Belsen was divided up into all sorts of different sections. We perhaps didn't see it straight away when-- when we got there, but we went into a section of about 12 barracks. And we were allocated to barracks according to male and female. This time, Rudy was with us, in the male, with my father and me. Eve and my mother in another barrack. We were in Belsen in one of the very few brick-built barracks that they had there-- very large barracks which, presently, is being excavated, actually. It's about 120 feet long and was divided into several sections. We were in section D, it was barrack number 11, and we were in section D. And the chief in our barrack was called [? Pinna, ?] and from Holland, originally German refugee, I think. But this camp that we were in then, this section of Belsen, was called the [GERMAN] lager, which means the exchange camp. So they were all people that had the blue stamps from Westerbork, but there were already other people in the camp-- especially there were some Greeks in the camp, very strange why they should be there, from Salonika. And in particular, a man called [INAUDIBLE], and he was the chief of the star camp, because he got there first. And there was Dutchman called [? Joseph Weiss. ?] He became his deputy, because the vast majority of people in the star camp were from Holland-- not necessarily Dutch, because many of them were German refugees like us. But the vast majority came from Holland, and Dutch was the main language that was spoken in the star camp. Eventually, more than 4,000 people lived in this star camp, in about 12 barracks. So I don't know how many people that is per barrack but quite a lot. It was pretty congested. And-- and the conditions, generally, were far worse than in Westerbork. It was nothing like Westerbork-- it was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were wooden, three-tiered barracks again, but we had far less room, I would say. And I can't remember that we have heating-- we had heating, as I remember it, in-- in the barracks, and a stove in Westerbork. I-- I don't remember that we had that even in Belsen-- might have done. But generally, the toilet facilities, washing facilities, all those kind of things were far worse. They were reasonably normal in Westerbork. But in Belsen, for example, the latrines, as they called them, were basically holes in the ground, at best there might have been some sort of wooden framework around it. But that was all, and it was inside a hut, so it was not all out in the open. But holes in the ground, basically, no flushing or anything like that. And there was a kind of a bath house where occasionally we did have showers in Belsen, but only very rarely. So generally, facilities were very much worse in-- in this-- our section of the star camp. And we were supposed to be privileged people. There were many other camps in Belsen, and some of those were also privileged. There was a camp for diamond people. There was a camp for Hungarian Jews, also for exchange. There was a camp for people with neutral connections. But-- and there was a very big camp for Russian prisoners of war, and we had nothing to do with those-- and many of those died. And later on, we had a very big camp for women who came from Auschwitz and the camps from the east when the Russian started to overrun that area. But we were in our particular section, we kept ourselves to ourselves. We were not allowed out, really. My parents had to work there, and the work was much worse. My father worked in what they called a shoe kommando, where they had a big tent, and they accumulated lots of shoes from all over Germany. And the job of these people was to take the shoes apart and salvage the leather. The Germans were great ones for salvaging everything, because they were an island nation, in a way, kind of thing. Cut off all the way around, kind of thing, and they needed everything they could get, in way of materials and rubber and all that sort of thing. And so they wanted to salvage all the leather on the shoes, and that was a job in this Belsen shoe kommando. My father worked in that. He was obviously totally unsuitable for that sort of work, having been a musician and a bank manager, all kinds of things. And not really suitable for that sort of practical work, so obviously didn't enjoy it. I don't know what my mother did-- presumably maybe worked in the kitchens. Many of the women worked in the kitchen, peeling potatoes, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not have to work in Belsen. I can't remember quite distinctly, although we didn't-- don't have any documents from Belsen at all. Well, we have documents afterwards, saying that we were in Belsen, but in Belsen, itself, there were no documents. The main thing from my point of view, being privileged in Belsen-- and-- and-- and we do recognize, now, that we did have a privileged existence in Belsen, compared with many other people who I've met after the war, and who are survivors who were in Belsen-- especially those who came from Auschwitz-- who had a far harder time than we had in Belsen. But we were privileged. But nevertheless, it wasn't exactly a holiday. But we were allowed to keep our own clothes, for example. And we had our own clothes with the yellow star on it-- so that was obviously a lot better than the Auschwitz women who were all in their striped, zebra uniforms, pajamas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you taken any possession with you from Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we could take all our possessions from Westerbork with us. And we have other clothes, and we had books, and I believe many of the Orthodox Jews had Sefer Torahs and Megillahs and all sorts of praying equipment, and everything. And they practiced all those sort of things whenever they could in Belsen. We saw that, and that's really the first time, I should think, that we saw these kind of habits, and learned about them and heard them praying, and-- and began to understand it all, really, and copy it, I think. So I suppose it was an education for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You copied it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think we did it, as well. When everybody else did it, we did it, as well. I think sort of there were benefits of being at Belsen. Basically, in a typical day in Belsen, that the first thing that happened in the morning was the appel, which is basically the roll call. So we all had to come out in this assembly yard, very early in the morning, something like 5:00 or 6:00 in the morning, to be counted. So we had to stand in rows of five. And-- and we would stand there whilst the Jewish elders-- not even [INAUDIBLE], himself, but his underlings-- we didn't have kapos, like they have these, and brutal people that were set on in Auschwitz and all the other concentration camps to administer laws. I don't remember that we had kapos in our section, but we had Jews who, again, administered all the rules that the SS laid down. It wasn't really a Jewish council-- as I said, it was Albala who was chief in our star camp, and [? Weiss ?] was his deputy. And they had people under them who would organize the counting. Because the counting was very difficult. First of all, they had to count almost 4,000 people standing there early in the morning, and that alone was difficult. But then, certain people were excused from coming to the appel-- people who had young children below a certain age, these women were allowed to stay in their barracks. So they had to be counted in their barracks with these young children. And then there were people who felt ill, and they would come out. So they had to be counted in their barracks, and there were 12 barracks. Then there were people who were in the hospital, they had to be counted in hospital. And all these separate counts, in the end, had to come to exactly the right number-- 4,174-- which the SS said that should be there. And if you didn't get it to 4,174, we had to stay there whilst they went around again, to check it all again, to see where they'd missed somebody. Some people were out on duties outside the camp. There was an SS man-- the only SS man that we ever remember-- called [? Roy ?] [? Muller, ?] Red [? Muller-- ?] because he had red hair-- so that was why we remember this SS chap. And he was in charge of the sewers and the ditches and the drainage. And he had a number of Jews with them who helped him. And they would go out, early the morning, before the appel. So they had to check out how many had gone with Red [? Muller ?] to check the drains. And-- and all these people had to be counted, and the count had to be matched right, according to the figure that the SS said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why they did the counting, nobody really knows. But that was-- that was a habit which apparently they had in most of the camps, that everybody was counted in the morning. Maybe to check that nobody had escaped, but in our camp, nobody ever escaped. You know, never-- they never thought about escaping-- it was far too difficult and risky, because we all thought we were going to be exchanged anyway. So why risk escaping? So nobody escaped. But very often, they didn't get the count right. So had to stand there, and-- and we stood there for hours and hours-- five, six hours, all day, sometimes. And-- not only in the sun, because we were in Belsen for more than a year-- from first of February '44, until April '45. So more than a year in Belsen. And every day we had this counting business. And it was hot in the summer, and it was cold in the winter, and it was raining, and snowing, and it was hailing, and it was slush.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've been back to Belsen several times-- once in the winter, and-- and-- when it was slush. And I was in my warm overcoat, and the wind was whistling across the heathland there in Belsen, kind of thing. And I felt drained and cold after five minutes out there, just like that. How we survived in those days, with the limited clothes that we had, I never know. But I guess we just got used to it. But that was a big ordeal every day to have this appel and the counting. When that was finished, we had the first meal of the day, as they called it, which was brown liquid, which they said was coffee-- ersatz-coffee, imitation coffee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was brown, and it was liquid, and it was warm, that's all you could say for it, really. And that's all we had in the morning. Nothing else-- no bread, or corn flakes, or milk, or anything like that. At lunch time, we had turnip soup. We had a mug full of turnip soup. We'd all have to queue up, and somebody would dish out the soup. In our case, it was my brother Rudy, the entrepreneur. He was in charge of dishing out the food, you see. He'd wrangled his way in there, kind of thing. And I'm hesitant to saying all these kind of things, because in a way, Rudy ought to tell better the story, himself. But there were a number of potatoes in each large urn that they had-- [DUTCH] as they call them in Dutch-- large urn, which the soup was-- you know, Rudy and his friends, they would go over to the kitchen outside the camp, and they'd bring over this urn. And then we'd all have to queue up, and he'd dish out the thing. And everybody would, of course, say, you know, stir it before you give it to us, because otherwise you would just get the water. But anyway, he could stir it, and he could still give everybody just the water. And he could keep the bits of potato and turnips in the bottom for his friends and relatives, if you like, kind of thing. He-- he knows he did all these sort of things, he's not particularly proud of them. And-- and in-- in some ways it's difficult to defend doing things like that, except that you got the mentality of everybody for themselves in-- in the camp-- where everybody tried to do whatever they could to stay alive, basically. The same-- which was in the evening, we would get black bread, about an inch and a half-- about that thick sliced bread. Basically, we had a big piece of black bread, and that was supposed to be for eight people. So first of all, it was tricky cutting it into eight equal pieces. Everybody would demand that they had the first choice, kind of thing, of each piece, kind of thing. And so there was a lot of chicanery going on with that. And then you could to do things where Rudy, me, and my father, we would say, we want a three-- we want a piece for three. We don't want it cut, because you lose a certain amount of bread whenever you cut it, you see. So we want a piece for three. And then if we didn't eat it, because we had stored some, we could give it back next day, and say, I want a piece for six. And we'd give back the three piece, and we might have shaved off half a millimeter of it. And-- it-- it was, yeah, Rudy was an entrepreneur and a scavenger, and he was an ideal man to have on your side. I think another there, I ought to say it this time, is that I probably owe-- maybe my life, even-- a lot to Rudy. Not only in remembering everything, but also in-- in pulling me through during my time in-- in Belsen and afterwards. And I've found since that many survivors have survived in pairs and in couples and in-- in groups. Very few people have survived entirely on their own. Most people have had somebody else that they egged each other on, if you like, in a way to survive, and not to give up, and to carry on and-- and do things, and work together and help each other. There were many people like that, which I could cite, but Frank-- Anne Frank--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other ways, apart from the food, where Rudy helped you and egged you on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time in Belsen, I don't think he would, because I didn't have to work. And-- and really there wasn't anything else, except the food and the appel-- he couldn't help me with the appel. We all had to stand there. But he-- he was punished several times because people complained about what he was doing-- giving them all the water, and all that. And he was punished several times, and there was solitary confinement. And the Jews, themselves, had to administer the punishment. And he admits that he went into solitary confinement on many occasions, and he was in there for two or three days. But he says it wasn't much different from life anyway-- he didn't get much to eat anyway. So-- for two or three days to be on his own in-- in some cubicle or whatever else, he said wasn't much different. Then he came back, and he would get the same, he would get his job back again. And he did the same again, and he would be sent to solitary confinement again. And it was just a regular thing. He didn't think much of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any news at this time about what was going on in the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a sort of underground-- obviously, we had no radios, newspapers, or anything like that. But because there were people who were in contact with the SS-- there was a sort of underground, and we knew what was happening. For example, your friend, [? Garwood ?] Garfunkel, his father, the barber, cut the hair of the SS, so he would obviously be able to maybe listen to radio, or find out. But anyway, yes, we-- we knew what the situation was in the outside world. That the Allies were winning. We could see the planes overhead-- at first only at night, later on during the day, and the occasional fighters attacking the bombers. So we could see that the war was going in-- in our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So during 1944, things weren't really too bad for us the first six months or so in-- in Belsen. But then, the hunger and starvation and the illnesses-- dysentery, and all that sort of thing that people had began to get worse. And there was no-- there wre hospitals, but there was no doctors, or medicines, or-- or anything like that. So people didn't really get better. And-- and it was really later in 1944 when things really began to get worse, when all these people from the east-- from the camps that were being overrun by the Russians-- came to Belsen, including the commandant, Josef Kramer, SS captain Josef Kramer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had been the commandant at Auschwitz. And all this team of SS people-- Irma Grese, the woman-- they all came to Belsen in December, 1944, and Belsen became a real concentration camp, and things really got worse.
